title: Restore Plant DB shortcut from welcome state
description:
## Slice Summary
Fix the App Command Graph navigation regression where the Plant Database shortcut no longer opens the side panel when no Design is open.

## User Value
Users can press Ctrl+2 from the welcome/no-Design state and reach the Plant Database as before.

## Out of Scope
Do not change the visual panel layout, shortcut definitions, or Design-dependent availability for unrelated panels.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Restore the Plant Database panel shortcut behavior from the welcome/no-Design state.

**Current behavior:**
The App Command Graph disables the Plant Database navigation command when no Design is open. As a result, pressing the Plant Database shortcut from the welcome state leaves the side panel closed, breaking the existing shortcut contract.

**Desired behavior:**
The Plant Database navigation command remains available from the no-Design state through keyboard shortcut dispatch and command execution. The command should still route through the App Command Graph, and any Design-dependent panel availability should remain deliberate and covered by tests.

**Key interfaces:**
- App Command Graph navigation commands
- Keyboard shortcut dispatch for panel navigation
- Shell panel and side-panel navigation state
- PanelBar disabled-state projection

**Out of scope:**
- Changing shortcut key assignments
- Changing the set of panels
- Reworking welcome-screen or shell layout
- Broadly changing Design-dependent availability beyond this regression
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Pressing the Plant Database shortcut from the no-Design state opens the Plant Database side panel.
- [ ] `runAppCommand()` preserves the intended Plant Database navigation behavior from the no-Design state.
- [ ] Existing shortcut contract coverage passes, including the panel shortcut test that exercises Ctrl+2.
- [ ] Focused frontend regression tests cover the no-Design Plant Database shortcut/command behavior.
- [ ] `cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit` passes.
close_reason: Restored no-design Plant Database command and shortcut behavior while preserving PanelBar disabled projection. Focused frontend tests and npx tsc --noEmit passed.

title: Cap Plant Spacing preview materialization
description:
## Slice Summary
Bound Plant Spacing preview work so very small valid intervals or long spacing guides cannot eagerly allocate and render an unbounded number of preview placed plants during pointer movement.

## User Value
A designer can enter physically valid dense intervals without freezing the interface while previewing a long spacing guide.

## Out of Scope
Do not block physically valid Plant Spacing Intervals outright, change the dense-confirmation threshold, or change final commit behavior beyond what is needed to keep preview work bounded.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Bound Plant Spacing preview generation before preview placed plants are materialized.

**Current behavior:**
Plant Spacing preview generation can eagerly compute every generated position on pointer movement before dense confirmation can protect the interface. A very small physically valid Plant Spacing Interval over a long spacing guide can allocate many positions and render one preview marker per generated placed plant.

**Desired behavior:**
Preview computation must derive the generated count without unbounded allocation and must cap or summarize preview markers before materializing them. Dense valid inputs remain allowed, but the live preview must stay responsive and communicate the dense count/warning rather than rendering unbounded ghost markers.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing sequence math
- Plant Spacing preview overlay
- Plant Spacing dense warning and dense confirmation
- Scene interaction pointer-move preview path

**Out of scope:**
- Blocking physically valid Plant Spacing Intervals solely because they are dense
- Changing the 100 generated-plant dense confirmation threshold
- Changing final dense commit behavior beyond any bounded-count helper needed by preview
- Adding new UX copy unless existing dense-warning copy is insufficient
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Live Plant Spacing preview does not allocate or render unbounded generated positions for very small valid intervals over long spacing guides.
- [ ] The preview still reports the generated count and dense warning for dense valid intervals.
- [ ] Normal previews at or below the safe preview cap still render generated ghost markers as before.
- [ ] Dense commits still require confirmation when generated count is greater than 100.
- [ ] Focused tests cover a tiny valid interval over a long spacing guide without materializing all generated positions.
close_reason: Bounded Plant Spacing live preview materialization with constant-time generated counts and capped ghost positions, deferred full dense position materialization until explicit confirmation, added focused preview-cap coverage, and verified with Plant Spacing sequence tests, SceneInteraction tests, and npx tsc --noEmit.

title: Block Plant Spacing HUD clicks from canvas commits
description:
## Slice Summary
Prevent Plant Spacing HUD pointer interactions from being interpreted as spacing guide endpoint clicks by the canvas.

## User Value
A designer can edit the Plant Spacing Interval or press Cancel/Confirm without accidentally creating placed plants or changing the current spacing guide.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Plant Spacing generation math, dense-count thresholds, keyboard shortcuts, or general canvas pointer handling outside Plant Spacing HUD interactions.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Prevent Plant Spacing HUD pointer interactions from becoming canvas spacing guide endpoint clicks.

**Current behavior:**
When Plant Spacing has a sampled placed plant, pointer interactions from the HUD can bubble into the canvas pointerdown handler. Editing the Plant Spacing Interval or pressing HUD actions can be treated as a spacing guide endpoint click before the control action runs.

**Desired behavior:**
Pointerdown interactions that originate inside the Plant Spacing HUD or its editable controls are consumed as HUD interactions only. They must not commit, update, or replace a Plant Spacing spacing guide endpoint. Canvas pointerdown interactions outside the HUD continue to drive Plant Spacing normally.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Spacing Tool HUD
- Canvas pointer interaction handling
- Plant Spacing Interval input
- Plant Spacing cancel and dense-confirmation controls

**Out of scope:**
- Changing Plant Spacing sequence math
- Changing dense-count confirmation behavior
- Changing non-Plant Spacing canvas tools
- Reworking general overlay architecture
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Clicking or pointerdowning the Plant Spacing Interval input does not create placed plants or commit a spacing guide endpoint.
- [ ] Clicking Plant Spacing HUD Cancel does not first commit generated placed plants.
- [ ] Clicking dense Confirm or dense Cancel is handled once by the HUD and is not also interpreted as a canvas endpoint click.
- [ ] Canvas clicks outside the Plant Spacing HUD still sample a source placed plant or commit a spacing guide endpoint as before.
- [ ] Focused SceneInteraction or overlay tests cover HUD-origin pointer events.
close_reason: Stopped Plant Spacing HUD pointerdowns from bubbling into canvas endpoint commits, added focused SceneInteraction regression coverage for interval-input pointerdowns, and verified with the focused SceneInteraction test file plus npx tsc --noEmit.

title: Handle Location map initialization failures
description: LocationPanel currently constructs a MapLibre map without a component-level failure path. A macOS user hit a MapLibre stack when opening the location feature, likely from WebGL or style initialization failure. Add a regression test and keep the location panel usable when map creation fails.
acceptance_criteria: Opening the location panel does not trip the global ErrorBoundary when MapLibre map initialization throws. The panel shows a localized degraded-map message while search and navigation controls remain mounted. Focused frontend regression tests cover the failure path.
close_reason: Fixed the macOS location-panel crash path by allowing MapLibre blob workers through Tauri WebKit CSP fallback, adding LocationTab degraded-map handling for MapLibre initialization failures, and covering both paths with frontend regression tests. Verified with npx tsc --noEmit and npm test.

title: Deepen canvas query and design file composition seams
description: Improve long-term architecture health by moving canvas scene freshness behind the Canvas Query Surface and driving Design file save composition from generated field ownership instead of handwritten duplicate field knowledge.
design: Keep runtime interfaces as small as practical; expose framework-specific reactivity through a thin app/runtime adapter where needed. Use generated Design field ownership as the source for composition, with explicit shared-extra handling for guides.
acceptance_criteria: Production app code no longer imports runtime mirror revision signals directly; Canvas Query Surface exposes scene read freshness through its seam; Design file composition uses generated ownership metadata to merge document-owned, scene-owned, and shared fields; source-boundary and focused regression tests cover the new invariants.
close_reason: Shipped Canvas Query Surface revision signals for scene, plant-name, and viewport freshness; moved app callers off runtime mirror revisions; changed Design file save composition to use generated field ownership with explicit shared extra ownership; added source-boundary and regression coverage. Tests: cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit; cd desktop/web && npm test -- document-format-contract frontend-boundaries-sources planning-projection canvas-runtime-surfaces maplibre-canvas-surface canvas-map-surface-lifecycle; cd desktop/web && npm test -- src/canvas/runtime/scene-runtime.test.ts src/canvas/runtime/scene-runtime/transactions.test.ts consortium-sync-workflow budget-workbench budget-tab-hover budget-tab-export consortium-chart-hover; cd desktop/web && npm test.

title: Deepen Scene Edit canvas mutation seam
description: Move canvas-owned persisted state changes behind a runtime-owned Scene Edit module so plants, zones, annotations, object groups, Layers, plant species colors, and guides share one mutation path for undo/redo, dirty state, mirror projections, revisions, and render invalidation.
design: Long-term health preference: make Scene Edit the only canvas mutation module even if this requires moving more code and tests.
acceptance_criteria: Scene-owned Layer and guide writes enter through the canvas runtime command/edit seam; signal-backed Layer and guide state is projection or transient UI state; focused tests cover undo/redo, dirty state, save composition, and render invalidation; agent docs reflect the new seam.
close_reason: Scene-owned layer and guide mutations now flow through the runtime Scene Edit seam; projections no longer author document state. Verified with npx tsc --noEmit and full desktop/web npm test.

title: Deepen app command, template import, and species read modules
description: Refactor three selected architecture review candidates: deepen the app command graph shared by menu/palette/shortcuts, move Design Template import coordination into a workflow module, and deepen Species Catalog read behavior used by Site Adaptation.
design: Favor long-term locality and leverage over minimum diff. Preserve existing behavior while reducing leakage through small adapter seams.
acceptance_criteria: App command metadata and dispatch are centralized behind one module; Design Template import is coordinated outside Community catalog state and core Design Session actions; Site Adaptation no longer owns raw Species Catalog SQL/storage facts for compatibility and replacement reads; focused tests cover each seam; relevant agent docs are updated or explicitly confirmed unchanged.
close_reason: Implemented App Command Graph, Design Template import workflow, and Species Catalog Read refactors; ran frontend typecheck, full frontend tests, rustfmt check, clippy, cargo check, and cargo test.

title: Deepen Species Catalog Workbench seam
description: Long-term architecture refactor from the architecture review: make the Species Catalog Workbench the public read/write module for Species Catalog UI behavior. The current Workbench protects components from some state imports, but its interface still mirrors raw search, favorites, dynamic option, detail selection, and view-mode implementation state.
design: Move Species Catalog UI state ownership into the Workbench implementation. Keep Preact ergonomics by exposing read-only reactive projections and commands from the Workbench. Stop treating app/plant-browser/state.ts as public surface; migrate components and tests away from raw writable signals except focused Workbench implementation tests.
acceptance_criteria: Species Catalog UI modules and broad tests use the Workbench interface rather than raw state exports. Workbench owns search intent/results, filter state, dynamic filter options, favorites, detail selection, view mode, and lifecycle. Source-boundary tests prevent plant-browser raw state from leaking into UI modules. Existing search, filtering, pagination, favorites, and detail-selection behavior is preserved. Required frontend typecheck and focused Vitest coverage pass.
close_reason: Implemented the Species Catalog Workbench as the public read/write seam, folded raw UI state and controller behavior into the Workbench factory/singleton, removed the old plant-browser state/controller public modules, migrated UI tests to Workbench interfaces, added boundary coverage, updated frontend agent docs, and verified with npx tsc --noEmit plus full frontend Vitest.

title: Deepen Design Session store seam
description: Long-term architecture refactor from the architecture review: make the Design Session module the read/write seam for active Design identity, dirty baselines, pending loads, saved markers, and attached/detached lifecycle state. Current Design Session behavior is deep, but tests and implementation still know too much about global signals in state/design.ts.
design: Introduce a Design Session store interface with a signal-backed adapter for production and a memory adapter for tests. Move writes for currentDesign/designPath/designName, dirty baselines, pending loads, detached canvas dirty, autosave failure, and saved markers behind the Design Session module. Preserve reactive read ergonomics through read-only projections instead of public writable globals.
acceptance_criteria: Design Session state-machine and persistence tests use a memory adapter rather than mutating global design signals directly. Production callers still get read-only reactive Design projections where needed. Existing document lifecycle behavior is preserved for attached and detached transitions, save/save-as, autosave, queued loads, and teardown snapshots. Boundary tests prevent new direct write access to Design Session internals. Required frontend typecheck and focused Vitest coverage pass.
close_reason: Implemented the Design Session store seam with signal-backed and memory adapters, migrated production callers off raw state/design imports, added a production boundary guard, updated document lifecycle agent docs, and verified with npx tsc --noEmit plus focused Vitest suites for Design Session, persistence, dirty state, document actions, and canvas document session integration.

title: Deepen Species Catalog SQL Planning
description: Architecture review found shallow Species Catalog SQL planning modules. The query builder splits useful concepts into modules, but the interface still leaks mutable parameter order, placeholder numbering, join alias ordering, count/list predicate convention, and string assembly details. Deepen Species Catalog SQL planning around a typed internal builder that owns placeholders, joins, predicate fragments, sort fragments, pagination, and shared count/list behavior.
design: Use a typed internal builder inside desktop/src/db/query_builder rather than ad hoc SQL strings passed through every module. Preserve the public SpeciesSearchRequest and SpeciesSearchPlan interface unless implementation proves a small compatibility facade is needed.
acceptance_criteria: SpeciesSearchPlan exposes the same search behavior while internal SQL assembly uses a typed plan/builder seam for placeholders, joins, predicates, order, limit, and count/list reuse. Existing backend search/filter/relevance tests pass or are strengthened. No runtime dependency is added. Rust formatting, clippy/check scope relevant to changed backend code is run or explicitly documented if blocked.
close_reason: Shipped typed query_builder SqlBuilder seam for Species Catalog SQL placeholder allocation, routed predicates/relevance/pagination/fixed filters through it, added dense-placeholder regression coverage, updated database agent guide, and passed cargo fmt --all -- --check, CANOPI_SKIP_BUNDLED_DB=1 cargo check --workspace, CANOPI_SKIP_BUNDLED_DB=1 cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test -p canopi-desktop db::query_builder, and cargo test --workspace.

title: Deepen Planning Canvas Workbenches
description: Architecture review found shallow planning canvas modules. Timeline Action and Consortium canvas workbenches currently require callers to understand renderer geometry, pointer ordering, local view state, Target presentation, popover lifecycle, and document edit transactions. Deepen the workbench modules so canvas hosts forward events and snapshots through a smaller seam while interaction implementation owns hover, selection presentation, drag preview and commit, popup orchestration, cleanup, and local view-state ordering.
design: Use separate deep modules first. Do not create a generic planning canvas abstraction until Timeline and Consortium prove the same interface. The deep modules own local view state involved in interaction correctness, including scroll, hover, pending click, popup state, drag lifecycle, auto-scroll, and cleanup.
acceptance_criteria: InteractiveTimeline delegates full Timeline Action interaction/view-state ordering to a deep app timeline workbench module. ConsortiumChart delegates full Consortium drag/hover ordering to a deep app consortium workbench module. Existing behavior is preserved with focused tests for hover, selection, drag commit/cleanup, popover open/save/delete, and no-op cleanup. Frontend typecheck and focused Vitest coverage pass.
close_reason: Deepened Planning Canvas Workbenches by moving Timeline and Consortium interaction ordering into app-layer workbench seams, updated boundary tests and agent docs, and verified with npx tsc --noEmit plus focused and full frontend Vitest suites.

title: Deepen Species Catalog search
description: Architecture review selected this as the second long-term-health refactor. Species Catalog search policy is currently spread across frontend search-session intent, IPC argument shapes, Tauri command arguments, plant_browser orchestration, user favorite hydration, plant_db search execution, and query_builder planning. Deepen this into a structured search module with flat transports as adapters.
acceptance_criteria: A structured Species Catalog search request/result contract replaces the internal flat argument chain; frontend search-session and IPC use adapter seams; Rust command/service/search modules consume the structured contract; favorite hydration and count policy are tested through the deeper interface; generated bindings are refreshed if shared contracts change; agent docs are updated only if commands or architecture guidance change.
close_reason: Deepened Species Catalog search: common-types now owns SpeciesSearchRequest, frontend search-session and IPC use a structured request with flat Tauri adapter, Rust command/service/plant DB/query planner consume the request object, favorite hydration and count-policy tests pass, generated bindings and agent docs were updated, and CI-parity gates passed.

title: Deepen Design file contract
description: Architecture review selected this as the top long-term-health refactor. The Design file contract is currently encoded across Rust shared types, generated TypeScript known keys, frontend field ownership metadata, scene serialization placeholders, and backend migration/default behavior. Deepen this into one authoritative contract so adding a Design field is no longer a cross-module memory test.
acceptance_criteria: common-types owns budget_currency and Design file defaults; generated frontend metadata includes known keys and field ownership; save/load composition tests cover ownership, defaults, and unknown fields; generated bindings are refreshed; agent docs are updated only if workflow commands or architecture guidance change.
close_reason: Deepened the Design file contract: common-types now owns budget_currency/defaults and field ownership metadata, generated frontend contract metadata is consumed by save composition, generated bindings were refreshed, tests and CI-parity gates passed.

title: Prepare 0.5.0 release
description: Bump app/package versions to 0.5.0, trigger the release candidate workflow, and leave release notes for after the GitHub release is created.
close_reason: Released Canopi 0.5.0 from RC run 26666890609, published release notes, and verified public GitHub release assets.

title: Fix macOS canvas object creation without randomUUID
description: Users on macOS 0.4.0 report they cannot add any canvas object. Older WKWebView builds may lack crypto.randomUUID, which is used by shared canvas object-creation paths. Add a deterministic repro and replace direct randomUUID calls with a compatible frontend ID helper.
close_reason: Fixed macOS canvas object creation by replacing direct frontend crypto.randomUUID calls with a createUuid helper that falls back to getRandomValues; added a SceneInteractionController regression for missing native randomUUID; verified npx tsc --noEmit and full npm test.

title: Collapse Timeline Action workbench
description:
Refactor the frontend Timeline Action workbench so InteractiveTimeline delegates add/edit/delete popover orchestration, selection cleanup, and document mutations through an app/timeline seam instead of owning those workflows inline. Preserve existing hover, drag, and document authority behavior.

## Required Skills
craft,improve-codebase-architecture
design: Create a deep app/timeline workbench module that coordinates Timeline Action form data, controller calls, popover mode, selection targets, and action mutations behind a small interface. Keep InteractiveTimeline responsible for canvas rendering, hit testing, pointer geometry, and refs.
acceptance_criteria: InteractiveTimeline no longer imports timeline controller mutations or form mapping helpers directly; Timeline Action add/edit/delete flows preserve behavior; boundary tests document the workbench seam; focused Timeline tests plus frontend typecheck pass.
close_reason: Collapsed Timeline Action workbench orchestration into app/timeline/workbench.ts; InteractiveTimeline now delegates popover action mutations and target cleanup through that seam; frontend typecheck and full Vitest suite passed.

title: Refactor Design Session into explicit state machine
description: Refactor the frontend Design Session lifecycle so guarded document transitions, attached/detached apply paths, queued loads, save composition, dirty baseline resets, autosave, teardown snapshots, and workflow installation are coordinated through an explicit state-machine module. Preserve existing document authority behavior and regression coverage.
design: Create a deep app/document-session state-machine seam. Keep DOM runtime ownership in lifecycle.ts and user action loading in actions.ts, but move transition ordering and session state into a single module with explicit state/status values and injected adapters for persistence/workflows/dialog/IPC.
acceptance_criteria: Design Session transitions preserve existing open/new/template/queued/mount behavior; attached and detached save paths preserve canvas/non-canvas authority; autosave and teardown use the state-machine surface; focused Vitest coverage documents state transitions and cancellation/error paths; docs/agent guidance is updated if the seam changes.
close_reason: Implemented explicit Design Session state machine; lifecycle now delegates start, autosave, and teardown through it; frontend typecheck and full Vitest suite passed.

title: Generate Species Catalog Filter adapter
description: Deepen the Species Catalog Filter module by moving repeated frontend/backend fixed-filter adapter behavior into generated metadata or generated adapter code, reducing parallel manual behavior across plant-filter-fields.json, TypeScript filter model code, Rust query_builder fixed-filter code, and shared SpeciesFilter request values.
acceptance_criteria: Fixed Species Catalog Filter behavior is generated or schema-driven on both TypeScript and Rust sides; request-value and SQL predicate behavior remain covered; generated bindings are current; frontend and Rust filter tests pass.
close_reason: Generated more Species Catalog Filter adapter behavior from common-types/plant-filter-fields.json: categorical strip fields now carry strip/chip UI metadata, frontend strip/chip catalogs consume generated behavior, and the Rust fixed SpeciesFilter request-value adapter is generated beside fixed-filter predicates. Verified generated bindings, focused filter tests, full frontend Vitest, TypeScript check, Rust format/check/clippy, and Rust workspace tests.

title: Deepen Design Session lifecycle
description: Refactor the Design Session lifecycle so attach, transition, save, autosave, teardown snapshot, dirty baselines, queued loads, and workflow installation are owned by a deeper app/document-session module instead of leaking ordering across the CanvasPanel hook, transition, persistence, and state modules.
acceptance_criteria: Design Session lifecycle has a single app-facing seam for canvas mount and autosave; existing document transition, persistence, dirty-state, queued-load, and canvas-session tests pass or are strengthened; no document authority rules are weakened.
close_reason: Deepened Design Session lifecycle by moving canvas runtime attachment, mount transition, autosave scheduling, queued-load installation, settings flush, teardown snapshot, persistence disposal, and canvas-session publication behind app/document-session/lifecycle.ts. Verified with focused lifecycle/boundary Vitest, full frontend Vitest, TypeScript check, Rust format/check/clippy, and Rust workspace tests.

title: Deepen Species Catalog Filter catalog
description: Why: Species Catalog Filter behavior is partly data-driven, but fixed-filter defaults, activity rules, presentation metadata, and SQL dispatch still require duplicated caller knowledge across JSON, generated TypeScript/Rust metadata, frontend model code, and backend query builder code. What: derive fixed-filter defaults, activity, UI behavior, and predicate dispatch from a single catalog seam while preserving the flat SpeciesFilter request shape unless a later bead deliberately changes IPC contracts.
design: Keep SpeciesFilter flat for compatibility. Deepen the catalog around it first, then decide separately whether the request contract should change.
acceptance_criteria: Fixed Species Catalog Filter behavior has one catalog interface for frontend defaults/activity/presentation and backend predicate dispatch; generated bindings remain source-controlled; focused frontend and backend tests cover catalog-derived behavior.
notes: Follow-up to closed canopi-9go. That bead moved fixed Species Catalog Filter presentation and Rust predicate behavior into generated metadata; this bead should focus only on remaining catalog shallowness such as frontend defaults/activity and any backend dispatch knowledge still duplicated after that work.
close_reason: Deepened the Species Catalog Filter catalog so frontend fixed-filter defaults and backend SQL dispatch derive from generated fixed-filter behavior, with focused frontend tests, query-builder coverage, full Rust workspace tests, typecheck, format, check, and clippy passing.

title: Deepen Design Session persistence module
description: Why: Design Session save, autosave, attached/detached persistence, teardown snapshotting, and field ownership composition are spread across several modules, so callers must preserve ordering knowledge. What: introduce a deeper Design Session persistence seam that centralizes persisted Design content production and snapshot behavior while preserving existing transition and dirty-state semantics.
design: Keep transition flow intact for this slice. Deepen the seam around producing persisted Design content and snapshotting canvas state, then update callers to consume that module instead of scattered runtime helpers.
acceptance_criteria: Persisted Design content for save/autosave/teardown goes through one Design Session persistence module; existing document transition, autosave, attached/detached snapshot, and field ownership behavior remains covered by focused tests; no unrelated architecture changes are included.
close_reason: Introduced app/document-session/persistence as the Design Session persistence seam, moved save/autosave/teardown callers to it, removed the old runtime helper, updated agent docs, and verified with focused Vitest, full frontend Vitest, and TypeScript checks.

title: Fix review-found correctness bugs
description: Fix the bugs found during static review: settings persistence must not mark failed saves as clean and close must flush settings; unknown .canopi top-level fields must round-trip through frontend saves; plant search cursor pagination must handle nullable sort values; document file actions must work when the canvas panel is unmounted; redo shortcut handling must accept shifted key events.
acceptance_criteria: Focused regression tests cover each fixed bug. Frontend typecheck passes. Rust workspace check passes for backend query changes.
notes: Implemented fixes for review-found correctness bugs: durable settings persistence/close flush, top-level .canopi unknown field preservation, nullable sort cursor pagination, detached document actions, and shifted redo shortcut. Verification passed: focused Vitest suite, full npm test, npx tsc --noEmit, cargo test query_builder, cargo test height_pagination_continues_after_null_sort_value, CANOPI_SKIP_BUNDLED_DB=1 cargo check --workspace, cargo test --workspace.
close_reason: Fixed all review-found correctness bugs and verified with focused/full frontend tests, TypeScript check, Rust workspace check, and Rust workspace tests.

title: Collapse registry to an App Command Graph facade
description:
## Slice Summary
Finish the App Command Graph deepening by reducing the public registry module to a facade over the internal graph implementation and updating affected guardrails.

## User Value
Callers keep a stable seam while maintainers can change the graph implementation with higher locality.

## Out of Scope
Do not broaden the command system, add new command categories, or perform broad source-ownership test cleanup beyond assertions touched by this refactor.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** chore
**Summary:** Reduce the public command registry module to an App Command Graph facade.

**Current behavior:**
The public App Command Graph module is both the caller-facing seam and much of the implementation. This makes internal refactors churn the same module that chrome, shortcut, and test guardrails import.

**Desired behavior:**
The public registry module is a small facade that re-exports or delegates to internal App Command Graph modules. Callers keep a stable seam, while the command catalog, projection adapters, and shortcut adapter remain implementation details. Any touched architecture guardrails should assert the seam rather than internal string details.

**Key interfaces:**
- App Command Graph public exports
- Command lookup and dispatch
- Chrome projections
- Keyboard handling
- Source ownership guardrails

**Out of scope:**
- New commands or command categories
- Broad source-ownership test cleanup not forced by this refactor
- Changing user-visible behavior
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] The public registry module primarily delegates to internal App Command Graph modules.
- [ ] Existing public exports remain available to current callers.
- [ ] App Command Graph source ownership tests are updated only as needed for the new facade shape.
- [ ] No chrome, shortcut, or panel caller imports internal graph implementation modules.
- [ ] Relevant frontend typecheck and focused command tests pass.
- [ ] Agent docs are updated if the App Command Graph seam guidance changes; otherwise the bead notes that no docs update was needed.
close_reason: Collapsed the public App Command Graph registry to delegate through the internal graph root, kept public exports stable, updated source guardrails and frontend agent guidance for the facade shape, and verified npx vitest run src/__tests__/frontend-boundaries-sources.test.ts src/__tests__/commands-registry.test.ts src/__tests__/shortcuts-manager.test.ts; npx tsc --noEmit; git diff --check.

title: Add a Design Session workflow runner
description:
## Slice Summary
Introduce a narrow Design Session workflow runner that owns install/dispose lifecycle for cross-authority workflows through explicit read/write seams.

## User Value
Future Design Session workflows can share one lifecycle path instead of adding bespoke module-level effects.

## Out of Scope
Do not create a plugin framework, change Design Session transitions, or move existing workflow behavior in this slice.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Add a narrow Design Session workflow runner for cross-authority workflow effects.

**Current behavior:**
Design Session lifecycle installs cross-authority workflow effects through a module-level workflow function. The lifecycle ownership is correct, but the workflow installation shape is bespoke and will not scale well as more Design Session workflows appear.

**Desired behavior:**
Design Session owns a workflow runner that installs and disposes workflow adapters through a narrow lifecycle interface. The runner should expose enough injected reads and writes for workflows to observe Design Session state, Canvas Query Surface freshness, and Design mutations without making every workflow import low-level authorities directly.

**Key interfaces:**
- Design Session workflow lifecycle
- Design Session read surface
- Canvas Query Surface read surface
- Design mutation seam
- Workflow disposer behavior

**Out of scope:**
- Moving Consortium sync behavior
- Adding new workflows
- Changing Design Session transition ordering
- Introducing a generic plugin framework
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Design Session has a narrow workflow runner with explicit install and dispose behavior.
- [ ] Workflow lifecycle remains owned by Design Session transitions and attached/detached session setup.
- [ ] The existing Consortium sync continues to install and dispose through the Design Session lifecycle, even if its behavior has not moved yet.
- [ ] Runner tests verify install idempotence and disposal behavior.
- [ ] No Design Session transition behavior changes for open, new, mount-existing, save, autosave, or teardown flows.
close_reason: Added a Design Session workflow runner with idempotent install/dispose behavior, wrapped existing Consortium sync as a workflow adapter, moved workflow disposal ownership from persistence to the Design Session state machine, updated guardrails and document lifecycle guidance, and verified focused workflow tests, npm test, npx tsc --noEmit, and git diff --check.

title: Project menus, palette, panels, and toolbar from command catalog
description:
## Slice Summary
Move App Command Graph chrome projection behavior onto the internal command catalog so each command definition drives menu, palette, panel, and toolbar projections consistently.

## User Value
All command chrome keeps the same behavior while future command additions require less duplicated projection work.

## Out of Scope
Do not change visible command grouping, ordering, labels, shortcuts, disabled states, or panel navigation semantics.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Drive App Command Graph chrome projections from the internal command catalog.

**Current behavior:**
Menu, command palette, panel, and toolbar projections are all exposed through the App Command Graph seam, but projection-specific metadata and projection construction are still coupled to the broad registry implementation.

**Desired behavior:**
Menu, palette, panel, and toolbar projections are built from the internal command catalog through internal projection adapters. Existing chrome callers keep the same public projection surfaces and visible behavior. Adding or changing a command should require changing fewer projection-specific lists.

**Key interfaces:**
- App Command Graph chrome projection
- App Command Graph panel projection
- App Command Graph toolbar projection
- Menu definition projection
- Command palette command projection

**Out of scope:**
- Changing visible chrome grouping or ordering
- Changing command labels or shortcuts
- Changing panel active/disabled semantics
- Moving shortcut matching
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Menu definitions are projected from the internal command catalog without changing visible menu content.
- [ ] Command palette entries are projected from the internal command catalog without changing palette command availability.
- [ ] Panel navigation projection is catalog-backed and preserves active/disabled behavior, including no-Design Plant Database behavior.
- [ ] Toolbar tool and action projections are catalog-backed and preserve active, pressed, and disabled behavior.
- [ ] Existing chrome projection tests pass or are updated to assert the same behavior through the public seam.
close_reason: Moved menu, palette, panel, and toolbar projection construction into an internal App Command Graph projection adapter, kept registry as the public seam, preserved chrome behavior, updated frontend agent guidance, and verified npx vitest run src/__tests__/frontend-boundaries-sources.test.ts src/__tests__/commands-registry.test.ts; npx tsc --noEmit; git diff --check.

title: Introduce internal App Command Graph catalog
description:
## Slice Summary
Create the first internal command catalog behind the existing App Command Graph seam while preserving all current public callers and behavior.

## User Value
Future command changes can be made in one place without re-teaching menus, palettes, toolbars, panels, and shortcuts about the same command identity.

## Out of Scope
Do not redesign command labels, shortcut assignments, menu structure, panel behavior, or toolbar layout.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Establish the internal command catalog behind the App Command Graph seam.

**Current behavior:**
The App Command Graph exists as the public seam for command identity, labels, availability, dispatch, shortcuts, and chrome projections. Its current implementation keeps many command facts and projection facts in one public-facing module, so the seam is useful but wider than it needs to be.

**Desired behavior:**
The public App Command Graph import path remains stable, but command identity, labels, availability, dispatch, and metadata live in an internal catalog module. Existing callers continue to consume the same public commands and projections. Behavior must remain unchanged.

**Key interfaces:**
- App Command Graph public exports
- Command identity and dispatch interface
- Command availability reads for Design Session, canvas command surface, shell state, and settings
- Existing command behavior tests

**Out of scope:**
- Changing visible command names, shortcuts, menu structure, panel behavior, or toolbar layout
- Moving chrome projection implementation
- Moving keyboard shortcut matching
- Broad architecture test cleanup
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] App Command Graph callers keep using the existing public import path.
- [ ] Command identity, labels, availability, and dispatch are sourced from an internal command catalog.
- [ ] Existing command behavior for file, view, navigation, theme, canvas tool, and canvas edit commands is unchanged.
- [ ] Focused tests cover catalog-backed command lookup, disabled state, and dispatch.
- [ ] Architecture guardrails still prevent chrome and shortcut callers from importing command implementation internals.
close_reason: Introduced internal App Command Graph catalog behind the public registry seam, preserved command behavior, added catalog boundary guardrails, updated frontend agent guidance, and verified npx vitest run src/__tests__/frontend-boundaries-sources.test.ts src/__tests__/commands-registry.test.ts; npx tsc --noEmit; git diff --check.

title: Move command shortcut matching into a graph adapter
description:
## Slice Summary
Move shortcut matching behind the App Command Graph so keyboard handling resolves to command identities instead of duplicating command actions.

## User Value
Keyboard behavior remains the same while shortcut rules gain locality and command dispatch remains consistent with chrome dispatch.

## Out of Scope
Do not change shortcut keys, editable-target suppression, canvas-only command availability, or command palette toggling behavior.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Put shortcut matching behind an App Command Graph adapter.

**Current behavior:**
Keyboard handling is routed through the App Command Graph, but shortcut matching still contains command-specific grammar and dispatch rules alongside the public registry implementation.

**Desired behavior:**
Shortcut matching lives in an internal App Command Graph shortcut adapter. The adapter resolves key events to command ids and delegates execution through graph dispatch. Context-sensitive canvas keyboard grammar remains explicit but no longer duplicates command actions.

**Key interfaces:**
- App Command Graph command id dispatch
- Keyboard shortcut handling seam
- Editable-target suppression
- Canvas command surface availability
- Command palette open/close state

**Out of scope:**
- Changing shortcut keys
- Changing command palette keyboard behavior
- Changing canvas-only shortcut availability
- Moving chrome projections
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Keyboard events resolve to App Command Graph command ids before dispatch.
- [ ] File, panel, canvas tool, view, edit, grouping, layer-order, and lock shortcuts keep their current behavior.
- [ ] Editable-target suppression remains intact.
- [ ] Command palette toggle and Escape behavior remain intact.
- [ ] Focused shortcut tests cover the adapter through the public App Command Graph keyboard seam.
close_reason: Moved App Command Graph shortcut matching and dispatch into an internal shortcut adapter, kept command-palette state on the public registry seam, added public shortcut-manager coverage for palette, view, edit, ordering, lock-or-unlock, and grouping shortcuts, updated frontend agent guidance, and verified npx vitest run src/__tests__/frontend-boundaries-sources.test.ts src/__tests__/commands-registry.test.ts src/__tests__/shortcuts-manager.test.ts; npx tsc --noEmit; git diff --check.

title: Move Consortium sync into a Consortium-owned workflow
description:
## Slice Summary
Move Consortium synchronization behavior behind a Consortium-owned workflow while Design Session keeps lifecycle ownership through the workflow runner.

## User Value
Consortium defaults and sync rules gain locality without changing Design Session lifecycle behavior.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Consortium Time Model semantics, add new sync workflows, or alter when Consortium entries are created.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Move Consortium synchronization behavior into a Consortium-owned Design Session workflow.

**Current behavior:**
Consortium synchronization is installed by Design Session workflow lifecycle but the sync behavior itself lives in the Design Session workflow module. That concentrates lifecycle but not Consortium domain knowledge.

**Desired behavior:**
Consortium synchronization behavior lives with the Consortium domain module as a Design Session workflow adapter. Design Session installs and disposes it through the workflow runner. The sync behavior still observes placed plants through the Canvas Query Surface and adds default Consortium entries through the Design mutation seam without marking the Design dirty.

**Key interfaces:**
- Design Session workflow runner
- Consortium sync workflow adapter
- Canvas Query Surface placed plant reads and scene revision freshness
- Design mutation seam
- Consortium Time Model default entry creation

**Out of scope:**
- Changing when default Consortium entries are created
- Changing Consortium Time Model rules
- Adding new planning sync workflows
- Broad source-ownership test cleanup
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Consortium sync behavior is owned by the Consortium module as a Design Session workflow adapter.
- [ ] Design Session installs and disposes the Consortium workflow through the workflow runner.
- [ ] Adding placed plants still creates missing default Consortium entries without marking the Design dirty.
- [ ] Existing Consortium sync tests pass or are updated to exercise the workflow through the runner seam.
- [ ] Workflow disposal remains HMR-safe and does not leak duplicate effects.
- [ ] Agent docs are updated if Design Session or Consortium workflow guidance changes; otherwise the bead notes that no docs update was needed.

title: Replace brittle architecture source assertions
description:
## Slice Summary
Reduce brittle source-text architecture assertions touched by the App Command Graph and Design Session workflow refactors, favoring import-rule checks and seam-behavior tests.

## User Value
Architecture guardrails keep protecting seams while internal refactors trigger fewer false failures.

## Out of Scope
Do not rewrite the entire source-ownership test suite or change architecture rules that are not touched by the preceding refactors.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** chore
**Summary:** Replace brittle architecture source assertions touched by the command and workflow refactors.

**Current behavior:**
Some architecture guardrails assert exact source text or implementation helper names. These checks protect useful seams, but they can become shallow modules when internal refactors fail tests despite preserving the interface.

**Desired behavior:**
Architecture guardrails touched by the App Command Graph and Design Session workflow refactors should prefer forbidden import rules, public seam behavior, and exported-interface checks over exact implementation string assertions. The guardrails should keep protecting the same architecture rules while allowing internal module movement.

**Key interfaces:**
- App Command Graph source ownership rule
- Design Session workflow source ownership rule
- Planning and Target Presentation source ownership rules when touched by dependency updates
- Public seam behavior tests

**Out of scope:**
- Rewriting unrelated architecture tests
- Weakening architecture rules
- Removing guardrails without replacement
- Changing production behavior
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Source ownership tests touched by the command refactor assert public seam and import rules instead of exact internal helper strings where practical.
- [ ] Source ownership tests touched by the workflow refactor assert lifecycle ownership and domain ownership without locking internal helper names.
- [ ] Any removed brittle assertion is replaced by an equivalent or stronger import-rule or behavior assertion.
- [ ] The architecture guardrail suite still catches direct imports across the App Command Graph and Design Session workflow seams.
- [ ] Relevant frontend tests and typecheck pass.

title: Keep active no-design Plant DB panel button enabled
description:
## Slice Summary
Fix the PanelBar projection regression where Plant Database can be opened from the welcome/no-Design state via shortcut but its active PanelBar button remains disabled.

## User Value
Users who open the Plant Database from the welcome state with Ctrl+2 can close it again from the visible active PanelBar button instead of being trapped in an active-but-disabled side panel state.

## Out of Scope
Do not change shortcut assignments, welcome-screen layout, Plant Database contents, Favorites behavior beyond shared panel chrome invariants, or Design-dependent availability for inactive panel entry points.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Keep the active Plant Database PanelBar button clickable when Plant Database is open without a Design.

**Current behavior:**
The Plant Database navigation command can open the Plant Database side panel from the welcome/no-Design state, but the PanelBar projection still marks the Plant Database chrome as disabled because no Design is open. The visible Plant Database button can therefore be both active and disabled, so users cannot click it to close the side panel.

**Desired behavior:**
When a side panel is already active, its PanelBar chrome remains enabled enough for the user to toggle it closed, even if the same panel entry point would be disabled while inactive in the no-Design state. Inactive no-Design entry points that are intentionally Design-dependent should remain disabled. Keyboard shortcut dispatch and command execution should keep their existing no-Design Plant Database behavior.

**Key interfaces:**
- App Command Graph panel projection
- PanelBar button disabled and active state
- Shell panel and side-panel navigation state
- Plant Database keyboard shortcut and command dispatch

**Out of scope:**
- Changing panel shortcut assignments
- Changing the welcome-screen layout
- Changing Plant Database or Favorites content
- Reworking side-panel toggle semantics beyond active panel chrome availability
- Broadly enabling unrelated Design-dependent inactive panel entry points
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Opening Plant Database from the no-Design state via the existing shortcut still opens the Plant Database side panel.
- [ ] While Plant Database is open in the no-Design state, the Plant Database PanelBar button projects as active and enabled.
- [ ] Clicking the active Plant Database PanelBar button in the no-Design state closes the side panel.
- [ ] Inactive no-Design Design-dependent panel entry points that should remain unavailable still project as disabled.
- [ ] Focused frontend regression tests cover the active no-Design Plant Database PanelBar click path.
- [ ] `cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit` passes.
close_reason: Kept active no-design Plant Database PanelBar chrome enabled so users can click it closed, while inactive no-design design-dependent entries remain disabled. Focused PanelBar/command/shortcut tests, full npm test, and npx tsc --noEmit passed.

title: Keep side panels open when toolbar tools change
description:
## Slice Summary
Fix the App Command Graph toolbar regression where choosing a canvas tool closes an already-open side panel.

## User Value
Users can keep Plant Database or Favorites open while switching canvas tools from the toolbar.

## Out of Scope
Do not rework toolbar layout, Plant Color behavior, panel toggle semantics, or Scene Edit runtime behavior.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Preserve open side panels when CanvasToolbar tool commands change the active tool.

**Current behavior:**
CanvasToolbar tool actions run through the App Command Graph. Tool dispatch currently navigates to the canvas panel before setting the tool, which closes an already-open Plant Database or Favorites side panel.

**Desired behavior:**
Toolbar tool selection keeps existing side panels open when the active primary panel is already the canvas. Tool dispatch should still route through the App Command Graph and still navigate to canvas when a tool command is invoked from a non-canvas primary panel.

**Key interfaces:**
- App Command Graph toolbar projection
- Canvas tool command dispatch
- Shell panel and side-panel navigation state
- CanvasToolbar click and roving-keyboard tool selection behavior

**Out of scope:**
- Changing CanvasToolbar visual design
- Changing Plant Color menu behavior
- Changing side-panel toggle semantics
- Reworking Scene Edit runtime tool behavior
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Clicking a CanvasToolbar tool while Plant Database or Favorites is open keeps that side panel open.
- [ ] Arrow-key toolbar tool navigation preserves the open side panel.
- [ ] Tool commands still set the active canvas tool and still navigate to canvas when invoked from a non-canvas primary panel.
- [ ] Focused frontend regression tests cover side-panel preservation for toolbar tool selection.
- [ ] `cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit` passes.
close_reason: Preserved open side panels for toolbar click and arrow-key tool changes while keeping tool commands routed to canvas from non-canvas panels. Focused tests, full npm test, and npx tsc --noEmit passed.

title: Route PanelBar through the App Command Graph
description:
## Slice Summary
Move PanelBar navigation availability and dispatch through the App Command Graph.

## User Value
Panel navigation keeps the same visible behavior while panel chrome stops reading Design Session state and shell routing details directly.

## Out of Scope
Do not change the visual layout, panel set, or side-panel resize behavior.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Route PanelBar availability and dispatch through the App Command Graph.

**Current behavior:**
PanelBar renders panel buttons and reads Design Session state directly to decide whether Design-dependent panels are disabled. It also dispatches panel navigation directly through shell routing state, so panel chrome has to know both availability state and navigation mechanics.

**Desired behavior:**
PanelBar consumes an App Command Graph projection for panel navigation commands. The projection supplies labels, disabled state, active or pressed state where needed, and dispatch. PanelBar remains responsible for layout and icons only. Location, Species Catalog Workbench, favorites, and canvas navigation behavior remains unchanged.

**Key interfaces:**
- App Command Graph panel-navigation projection
- Shell panel navigation command dispatch
- Design Session availability read behind the command graph seam
- PanelBar rendering contract

**Out of scope:**
- Changing the set of panels
- Changing side-panel toggle semantics
- Changing side-panel resize behavior
- Changing panel visual design
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] PanelBar uses App Command Graph projection data for panel labels, disabled state, active state, and dispatch.
- [ ] PanelBar no longer imports Design Session state only to determine whether panel buttons are available.
- [ ] Canvas, Location, Species Catalog Workbench, and favorites navigation preserve current toggle and disabled behavior.
- [ ] Focused frontend tests cover no-Design disabled state and side-panel toggle behavior through the command graph seam.
- [ ] `cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit` passes.
close_reason: Routed PanelBar through the App Command Graph panel projection, centralized panel disabled/active/dispatch state including favorites, preserved panel chrome labels, added command projection, PanelBar behavior, and source-boundary tests, and verified focused Vitest plus frontend typecheck.

title: Move terrain and Target Presentation inputs into the Canvas Map Surface seam
description:
## Slice Summary
Finish the Canvas Map Surface deepening by moving terrain and Target Presentation snapshot assembly behind the same seam.

## User Value
Contours, hillshade, and hovered or selected Target overlays keep working while map visualization inputs have one owner.

## Out of Scope
Do not change terrain data sources, map projection math, or Target identity semantics.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Move terrain and Target Presentation input assembly into the Canvas Map Surface seam.

**Current behavior:**
After core map inputs are moved, terrain layer settings and Target Presentation overlay data may still be assembled by the mounted map surface controller. That leaves the Canvas Map Surface snapshot split between the seam and its caller.

**Desired behavior:**
The Canvas Map Surface owns the complete MapLibre-ready snapshot, including contour interval, contour visibility and opacity, hillshade visibility and opacity, theme-specific terrain paint inputs, hovered Targets, and selected Targets. The mounted surface remains a DOM attachment and state-reporting adapter only. Contours, hillshade, hovered Target overlays, and selected Target overlays preserve current behavior.

**Key interfaces:**
- Canvas Map Surface snapshot seam
- Terrain settings projections
- Target Presentation overlay snapshot
- MapLibre terrain sync interface
- MapLibre overlay sync interface

**Out of scope:**
- Changing terrain source URLs or protocols
- Changing Target identity or resolution semantics
- Changing map/canvas projection math
- Changing Target Presentation ownership
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Canvas Map Surface module assembles terrain and Target Presentation inputs into the same snapshot seam as core map inputs.
- [ ] Mounted MapLibre canvas surface no longer reads terrain settings or Target Presentation overlay signals directly.
- [ ] Contours, hillshade, terrain opacity/theme updates, hovered Target overlays, and selected Target overlays preserve current behavior.
- [ ] Focused frontend tests cover terrain and Target Presentation snapshot assembly and lifecycle update inputs.
- [ ] `cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit` passes.
close_reason: Moved terrain settings and Target Presentation overlay inputs into the Canvas Map Surface snapshot seam, reduced the mounted controller to lifecycle update with the full snapshot, added snapshot and source-boundary coverage, updated MapLibre agent docs, and verified focused Vitest plus frontend typecheck.

title: Route CanvasToolbar through the App Command Graph
description:
## Slice Summary
Move CanvasToolbar command state and dispatch through the App Command Graph.

## User Value
Canvas tools and toolbar actions keep working while toolbar command identity, labels, active state, disabled state, and dispatch are concentrated behind one seam.

## Out of Scope
Do not change plant color picker internals or Scene Edit runtime behavior.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Route CanvasToolbar command state and dispatch through the App Command Graph.

**Current behavior:**
CanvasToolbar partially uses App Command Graph commands for undo and redo, but still assembles most toolbar state locally from canvas command/query signals, canvas selection mirrors, tool state, grid/snap/ruler signals, locale, and local tool definitions. This keeps command identity and availability spread across the toolbar and command graph.

**Desired behavior:**
CanvasToolbar consumes an App Command Graph toolbar projection for tool commands and shared toolbar actions. The projection supplies command labels, shortcuts, disabled state, pressed or active state where applicable, and dispatch. CanvasToolbar remains responsible for vertical toolbar layout, icons, tooltips, and rendering the Plant Color menu anchor. Plant Color picker internals may remain local unless the command projection needs a minimal plant color action entry.

**Key interfaces:**
- App Command Graph toolbar projection
- Canvas command surface and tool state behind the command graph seam
- Canvas settings toggles for grid, snap, and rulers behind the command graph seam
- CanvasToolbar rendering contract

**Out of scope:**
- Reworking the Plant Color picker internals
- Changing Scene Edit runtime behavior
- Changing toolbar visual design or icon assets
- Changing shortcuts or command labels
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] CanvasToolbar uses App Command Graph projection data for canvas tool commands and shared toolbar actions.
- [ ] Tool active state, undo/redo disabled state, and grid/snap/ruler pressed state preserve current behavior.
- [ ] Toolbar command dispatch still navigates to canvas when tool commands require it.
- [ ] Plant Color menu behavior remains unchanged or is explicitly left behind its existing local seam.
- [ ] Focused frontend tests cover toolbar projection state for tool selection, undo/redo availability, and settings toggles.
- [ ] `cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit` passes.
close_reason: Routed CanvasToolbar tool, history, and grid/snap/ruler command state through the App Command Graph toolbar projection while leaving Plant Color on its existing local seam; added projection, UI, and source-boundary tests; updated the frontend agent guide; verified focused Vitest plus frontend typecheck.

title: Move core Canvas Map Surface inputs behind one seam
description:
## Slice Summary
Deepen the Canvas Map Surface seam so core map inputs are assembled inside the map surface module instead of the mounted Preact surface controller.

## User Value
In-canvas basemap Location, visibility, opacity, theme, bearing, and canvas camera sync continue to work while callers pass only the DOM attachment and state callback.

## Out of Scope
Do not move terrain layer inputs or Target Presentation overlays in this slice.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Move core Canvas Map Surface input assembly behind the Canvas Map Surface seam.

**Current behavior:**
The mounted map surface controller reads canvas query state, saved Location, base layer visibility, base layer opacity, theme, basemap style, and map bearing directly before calling the map lifecycle. The lifecycle is deep, but its caller still has to understand the authorities that produce the map snapshot.

**Desired behavior:**
The Canvas Map Surface owns core snapshot assembly for runtime, saved Location, basemap style, base layer visibility and opacity, theme, and map bearing. The mounted Preact surface attaches a DOM element, receives state updates, and does not assemble those core inputs. In-canvas basemap rendering, camera sync, precision diagnostics, and map readiness state remain unchanged.

**Key interfaces:**
- Canvas Map Surface snapshot seam
- Canvas query surface revisions
- Location Workbench saved Location presentation
- Canvas layer settings and app theme projections
- MapLibre lifecycle update interface

**Out of scope:**
- Terrain layer inputs
- Target Presentation overlays
- Map projection math
- Location draft/search behavior
- MapLibre tile or terrain source changes
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Canvas Map Surface module assembles core map snapshots for runtime, saved Location, basemap style, base layer visibility/opacity, theme, and bearing.
- [ ] Mounted MapLibre canvas surface no longer reads those core input signals directly.
- [ ] Basemap visibility, opacity, theme, bearing, camera sync, and readiness state preserve current behavior.
- [ ] Focused frontend tests cover core snapshot assembly and lifecycle update inputs.
- [ ] `cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit` passes.
close_reason: Moved core Canvas Map Surface input assembly into app/canvas-map-surface/snapshot.ts, updated the mounted controller to consume that seam while leaving terrain and Target inputs for the follow-up bead, added snapshot and source-boundary tests, updated MapLibre agent docs, and verified focused Vitest plus frontend typecheck.

title: Expose reactive App Command Graph chrome projections
description:
## Slice Summary
Deepen the App Command Graph so chrome callers can consume reactive command projections instead of directly subscribing to Design Session and canvas command state.

## User Value
Menu, palette, and shortcut behavior remains identical while command availability and dispatch knowledge concentrates behind one app command seam.

## Out of Scope
Do not migrate PanelBar or CanvasToolbar in this slice beyond any minimal hooks needed to prove the projection seam.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Deepen the App Command Graph by adding reactive chrome projections for command callers.

**Current behavior:**
The App Command Graph owns command identity, labels, dispatch, shortcuts, and menu projection, but mounted chrome still subscribes directly to Design Session and canvas state to keep command availability fresh. Menu and palette callers can use command helpers, but the seam does not yet provide one reactive projection surface that callers can consume without knowing low-level state sources.

**Desired behavior:**
The App Command Graph exposes reactive projections that let chrome render command labels, shortcuts, disabled state, and dispatch affordances without importing Design Session or canvas state only for freshness. Menu behavior, command palette behavior, and keyboard shortcut behavior remain unchanged. The project context vocabulary update for App Command Graph and Canvas Map Surface is carried with this slice.

**Key interfaces:**
- App Command Graph command identity, dispatch, shortcut, and projection interface
- Design Session read state used only behind the command graph seam
- Canvas command/query availability used only behind the command graph seam
- Menu and command palette projection consumers

**Out of scope:**
- Migrating PanelBar command routing
- Migrating CanvasToolbar command routing
- Changing command labels, shortcuts, menu structure, or palette contents
- Changing Design Session or canvas runtime behavior
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] App Command Graph exposes a reactive chrome projection interface for command labels, shortcuts, disabled state, and dispatch.
- [ ] Menu and command palette behavior remain unchanged while menu freshness no longer requires mounted chrome to import Design Session or canvas session state directly.
- [ ] Keyboard shortcut dispatch still routes through the App Command Graph and preserves editable-target guards.
- [ ] Project context includes the settled App Command Graph and Canvas Map Surface vocabulary.
- [ ] Focused frontend tests cover projection freshness for Design Session and canvas availability changes.
- [ ] `cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit` passes.
close_reason: Exposed reactive App Command Graph chrome projections, migrated MenuBar and CommandPalette to consume the projection, added focused projection and source-boundary tests, and verified focused Vitest plus frontend typecheck.

title: Deepen Species Catalog read projections
description: Deepen Species Catalog read projections so Site Adaptation no longer carries Species Catalog SQL shape, placeholder construction, row mapping, or localized Common Name hydration. Search, detail, and Site Adaptation should consume projection-oriented Species Catalog read modules while Compatibility Check meaning remains in Site Adaptation.
design: Long-term decision: Species Catalog read projections are the storage seam. Site Adaptation owns Compatibility Check semantics and Replacement Suggestion response shaping, but not raw Species Catalog SQL or row hydration details.
acceptance_criteria: Species Catalog storage columns and localized Common Name hydration for Site Adaptation are owned by read projection modules; Site Adaptation keeps hardiness compatibility interpretation only; backend tests cover compatibility and replacement ordering through the new seam; Rust fmt, clippy/check, and relevant tests pass.
close_reason: Deepened Species Catalog read projections into compatibility, replacement, common-name, and SQL helper modules; kept Site Adaptation on interpretation/response shaping; moved storage test fixture to the read seam; updated domain/agent docs; verified focused tests plus Rust fmt/check/clippy/workspace tests.

title: Deepen Plant Stamp source seam
description: Deepen the Plant Stamp source seam so Species Catalog presentation modules do not serialize drag payloads or write plant stamp state directly. Species-to-Plant Stamp translation, drag data, selected stamp state, tool activation, and clear-on-deactivate should live behind one module interface.
design: Long-term decision: Plant Stamp source is an in-process seam because both click-to-stamp and drag-to-canvas are adapters for choosing the same Plant Stamp source. The Species Catalog Workbench stays a catalog interaction surface, not Plant Stamp authority.
acceptance_criteria: PlantCard and PlantRow no longer assemble Plant Stamp payloads or write plantStampSpecies directly; drag payload parsing/serialization is centralized; Plant Stamp tool consumes the new seam; focused frontend tests cover place action and drag payload behavior; frontend typecheck passes.
close_reason: Deepened Plant Stamp Source into canvas/plant-stamp-source.ts, centralized drag data and selected source lifecycle, updated Species Catalog callers/runtime tool/tests/docs, and verified focused Vitest plus frontend typecheck.

title: Move Consortium Time Model out of renderer
description: Deepen the Consortium time model seam so Succession Phase order, Stratum order, default Consortium entry rules, and clamping rules are owned outside the Canvas2D renderer. Renderer modules should consume the model instead of being the source of domain facts.
design: Long-term decision: the Consortium time model owns Succession Phase label/duration keys, Stratum order, default Stratum, and phase clamping. Canvas2D drawing owns only geometry and pixels.
acceptance_criteria: Consortium renderer no longer owns Succession Phase and Stratum constants; Design Session workflow and Consortium interaction import the model seam; focused frontend tests cover default entry and clamping/order behavior; frontend typecheck passes.
close_reason: Moved Consortium Time Model facts into app/consortium/time-model.ts, updated renderer/workflow/interaction callers and docs, and verified with focused Vitest coverage plus frontend typecheck.

title: Deepen Diagnostic Bundle assembly
description: Refactor Problem Report backend assembly so Diagnostic Bundle privacy policy, redactions, manifest and attachment assembly, backend log excerpt collection, and stored ZIP encoding live behind focused internal modules instead of one broad problem_report.rs implementation.
design: Convert services/problem_report.rs into a services/problem_report/ module directory. Keep create_problem_report and show_problem_report_folder as the public service seam. Keep the folder reveal adapter separate from Diagnostic Bundle assembly. Avoid new runtime dependencies; preserve the hand-rolled stored ZIP implementation behind a zip module.
acceptance_criteria: Problem Report public command behavior remains unchanged. Diagnostic Bundle assembly has focused internal modules for bundle content, redaction, ZIP encoding, and folder reveal. Privacy-sensitive tests continue to cover default exclusions and explicit current Design consent. Rust tests for problem reporting pass, and Rust formatting check passes.
close_reason: Refactored Problem Report backend assembly into focused modules for Diagnostic Bundle content, redactions, stored ZIP encoding, summary generation, and folder reveal validation while preserving create_problem_report/show_problem_report_folder behavior. Verified with cargo test -p canopi-desktop services::problem_report, cargo fmt --all -- --check, CANOPI_SKIP_BUNDLED_DB=1 cargo check --workspace, and CANOPI_SKIP_BUNDLED_DB=1 cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings.

title: Deepen Timeline Action Canvas module
description: Refactor the Timeline Action Canvas interaction so InteractiveTimeline crosses one small module interface while planning input assembly, render dependencies, event wiring, interaction frame assembly, popover projection, Target Presentation writes, and cleanup live behind the Timeline Action Canvas seam.
design: Use a timeline/canvas module directory with an index export for the host-model interface. Keep renderer purity and existing Timeline Action document edit modules intact. Treat interaction frame/controller files as implementation details of the Timeline Action Canvas module.
acceptance_criteria: InteractiveTimeline imports one Timeline Action Canvas module seam. Timeline Action canvas internals live under app/timeline/canvas rather than multiple sibling public-ish modules. Boundary docs and source guard tests describe the deeper seam. Focused frontend tests and TypeScript check pass.
close_reason: Shipped the Timeline Action Canvas deeper seam: InteractiveTimeline now imports app/timeline/canvas, canvas internals live under app/timeline/canvas, source guard tests and agent docs were updated, and focused Vitest plus TypeScript checks passed.

title: Guard stale Problem Report submissions
description:
## Slice Summary
Prevent older in-flight Problem Report submissions from writing success or error state into a freshly opened Problem Report dialog after the dialog has been closed, reset, or reopened.

## User Value
A user can close and reopen the Problem Report dialog while report creation is still pending without seeing stale success, error, or loading state from the previous submission.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Diagnostic Bundle contents, current Design opt-in behavior, report upload policy, Rust privacy filtering, or generated Problem Report artifacts.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Guard Problem Report submission state against stale async completions after close, reset, or reopen.

**Current behavior:**
Problem Report submission state is held in a persistent frontend module. A submit command awaits report creation and then writes result, error, show-folder state, and submitting state into shared signals. If the dialog is closed and reopened while an older report creation is still pending, the older completion can write into the fresh dialog state.

**Desired behavior:**
Only the currently active submission attempt may update submission result, error, show-folder, or submitting state. Closing, resetting, or reopening the dialog invalidates any older in-flight submission. Late success or failure from an invalidated attempt is ignored. Existing default privacy behavior remains unchanged: current Design contents are excluded unless the user explicitly opts in.

**Key interfaces:**
- Problem Report frontend submission module
- Problem Report dialog view model
- Diagnostic Bundle IPC adapter
- Current Design attachment opt-in seam

**Out of scope:**
- Changing Diagnostic Bundle contents or privacy exclusions
- Adding upload, telemetry, external issue creation, or support-channel behavior
- Changing Rust Problem Report assembly or folder reveal validation
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] A regression test covers a pending Problem Report submission that completes after the dialog is closed and reopened.
- [ ] Late success from an invalidated submit does not populate a fresh dialog's result or clear its current state.
- [ ] Late failure from an invalidated submit does not populate a fresh dialog's error or clear its current state.
- [ ] The active submit path still sets submitting state, handles success and failure, and preserves current Design explicit opt-in behavior.
- [ ] Focused Problem Report frontend tests and TypeScript checks pass.
close_reason: Guarded Problem Report submission state with a monotonic generation token so close, reset, reopen, and newer submits invalidate older async completions. Added regression coverage for stale success and stale failure after close/reopen, plus active failure behavior. Verified with focused Problem Report Vitest coverage and TypeScript check.

title: Move Problem Report submission behind one frontend module
description:
## Slice Summary
Move frontend Problem Report submission state and request assembly behind a dedicated module so the dialog renders state and invokes commands instead of assembling diagnostics, sensitive attachments, and IPC calls itself.

## User Value
Problem Reports keep their local-first Diagnostic Bundle behavior while privacy-sensitive request assembly becomes easier to verify and harder to bypass from UI code.

## Out of Scope
Do not add automatic upload, telemetry, external issue creation, new Diagnostic Bundle contents, or changes to Rust privacy filtering in this slice.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Move frontend Problem Report submission state and request assembly behind one module.

**Current behavior:**
The Problem Report dialog owns UI state and also assembles the Problem Report request. It reads recent frontend diagnostics, decides whether to build a current Design attachment, constructs sensitive attachment payloads, calls the IPC adapter, handles copy behavior, and reveals the generated folder. This gives the view too much knowledge of the local-first privacy interface.

**Desired behavior:**
The frontend Problem Report module owns submission state, request assembly, diagnostics capture, optional current Design attachment assembly, IPC submission, copy summary behavior, and generated-folder reveal behavior. The dialog consumes a view model and calls module commands. Current Design attachment assembly uses the Design Session document surface or injected equivalent so persisted content is composed the same way as a save. Rust remains the authoritative Diagnostic Bundle assembly and privacy-filtering module.

**Key interfaces:**
- Problem Report frontend module
- Problem Report request contract
- Diagnostic Bundle IPC adapter
- Frontend diagnostic recorder
- Design Session document surface
- Rust Problem Report assembly module

**Out of scope:**
- Adding automatic upload, telemetry, email drafts, or external issue creation
- Changing Rust Diagnostic Bundle privacy filtering or default exclusions
- Adding new sensitive attachment kinds
- Changing Design persistence semantics
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] The Problem Report dialog no longer directly assembles Problem Report request objects, frontend diagnostics, sensitive attachment payloads, or IPC calls.
- [ ] A frontend Problem Report module exposes state and commands for description edits, current Design opt-in, submit, copy summary, reveal folder, error handling, and close/reset behavior.
- [ ] Current Design attachment assembly remains explicit opt-in and is composed through the Design Session document surface or an injected equivalent.
- [ ] Default Diagnostic Bundles still exclude Design contents, precise Location, screenshots, and raw filesystem paths unless the current Design attachment is explicitly selected.
- [ ] Frontend tests cover dialog behavior through the public Problem Report module behavior rather than internal component state.
- [ ] Relevant agent docs are updated if Problem Report frontend seams or privacy invariants change.
close_reason: Moved frontend Problem Report submission state and request assembly into app/problem-report/submission, made the dialog render module state and call module commands, kept current Design attachment opt-in through the existing Design Session persistence attachment seam, updated Problem Reporting docs, and verified with focused Problem Report tests, full frontend Vitest, and TypeScript check.

title: Narrow Design Session operations to intent-shaped commands
description:
## Slice Summary
