Make Design Session callers use intent-shaped operations instead of constructing low-level transition recipes with source strings, dirty guard modes, load callbacks, queue behavior, and attached session facts.

## User Value
Opening, saving, autosaving, mounting, and tearing down a Design Session keep the same behavior while replacement policy gains locality behind one interface.

## Out of Scope
Do not redesign the Design file format, dirty-state model, canvas scene authority, or persistence composition rules in this slice.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Narrow Design Session callers to intent-shaped operations instead of low-level transition recipes.

**Current behavior:**
Design Session replacement behavior is centralized, but production callers can still construct low-level transition requests that include source identifiers, dirty guard modes, load callbacks, queue deferral behavior, attached session selection, and cancellation checks. This makes callers know policy details that should live behind the Design Session seam.

**Desired behavior:**
Production callers express Design Session intent through narrow operations such as creating a new Design, opening a Design from a known path, opening a template as an unsaved Design, mounting an attached Design Session, consuming queued loads, saving, autosaving, and tearing down. Low-level transition request construction becomes internal to the Design Session module or only a focused internal test surface. Existing attached and detached behavior, dirty guard ordering, queued load behavior, autosave behavior, workflow installation, and persistence composition remain unchanged.

**Key interfaces:**
- Design Session operation interface
- Design Session state machine
- Dirty replacement guard
- Design Session persistence composition
- Canvas document surface
- Queued Design load behavior

**Out of scope:**
- Changing the Design file format or migration behavior
- Changing which Design fields are canvas-owned or document-owned
- Changing autosave interval settings
- Changing current Problem Report submission behavior
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Production callers no longer construct low-level Design transition request objects.
- [ ] User-facing open, new, template import, save, save-as, autosave, queued-load, mount, and teardown behavior remains unchanged.
- [ ] Low-level transition source, dirty guard, attached/detached branching, queue restoration, and load callback ordering are local to the Design Session module.
- [ ] Tests cover the public intent-shaped operations and preserve existing dirty guard, queued load, attached mount, detached transition, save, autosave, and teardown behavior.
- [ ] No Design file ownership or persistence composition rules are weakened.
- [ ] Relevant agent docs are updated if Design Session operation names or caller conventions change.
close_reason: Moved open/new/template Design replacement recipes behind intent-shaped Design Session operations, removed low-level transition request construction from public document actions, kept state-machine transition details internal to the Design Session module and tests, updated document lifecycle docs, and verified with focused Design Session tests, full frontend Vitest, and TypeScript check.

title: Make canvas runtime surfaces real adapters
description:
## Slice Summary
Make the canvas runtime public seam consist of explicit command, query, and document adapters instead of exposing one mounted runtime that satisfies every canvas interface.

## User Value
App features that command, query, or persist canvas state can depend on a narrower interface, making future canvas and Design Session work safer to change and easier to test.

## Out of Scope
Do not change canvas user behavior, renderer behavior, scene edit semantics, Design Session policy, or Problem Report submission behavior in this slice.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Make the canvas runtime public seam consist of real command, query, and document adapters.

**Current behavior:**
The canvas public seam is documented as separate command, query, and document surfaces, but the mounted runtime still satisfies all three interfaces directly. The published surfaces mostly forward to that single object, and compatibility paths allow tests or callers to publish a mounted runtime or narrow fake surface directly.

**Desired behavior:**
Production code publishes explicit canvas runtime surfaces, and each surface is a concrete adapter over the private runtime implementation. The runtime implementation no longer needs to satisfy the combined mounted-runtime interface directly. App callers and broad tests consume command, query, or document surfaces according to their role. Any compatibility path that accepts a combined runtime is removed from production code or confined to narrow test helper behavior.

**Key interfaces:**
- Canvas command surface
- Canvas query surface
- Canvas document surface
- Canvas runtime surface publication seam
- Scene runtime implementation
- Design Session lifecycle canvas attachment

**Out of scope:**
- Changing renderer behavior, fallback behavior, or scene rendering output
- Changing Scene Edit semantics, undo/redo behavior, or dirty-state semantics
- Changing Design Session replacement policy
- Changing Problem Report behavior
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Production canvas session publication accepts explicit command/query/document surfaces rather than relying on a mounted runtime object satisfying every surface.
- [ ] The scene runtime implementation is wrapped by concrete command, query, and document adapters with no app caller receiving the combined runtime as its public interface.
- [ ] Test helpers that need fake canvas surfaces publish role-specific surfaces or explicit surface bundles.
- [ ] Existing canvas toolbar, Design Session lifecycle, Planning Projection, MapLibre, and plant color behavior remain unchanged.
- [ ] Focused tests cover surface separation and prevent query consumers from issuing commands or document lifecycle calls.
- [ ] Relevant agent docs are updated if canvas runtime publication or test-helper conventions change.
close_reason: Implemented explicit CanvasRuntimeSurfaces publication with concrete SceneCanvasRuntime command/query/document adapters, removed MountedCanvasRuntime publication compatibility, migrated test helpers to publish explicit surface bundles, updated canvas runtime agent docs, and verified with focused Vitest, full frontend Vitest, and TypeScript check.

title: Enforce Design Object Locks through Object Group membership
description:
## Slice Summary
Enforce the agreed invariant that an Object Group containing a locked Design Object cannot be moved, deleted, stamped, ungrouped, or otherwise edited in a way that mutates the locked member.

## User Value
A Design Object Lock consistently protects the object even when the user interacts with an Object Group that contains it.

## Out of Scope
This slice should not change the persistence contract created by the direct lock slice, and should not add visual lock indicators.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** feature
**Summary:** Enforce Design Object Locks when editing Object Groups that contain locked members.

**Current behavior:**
Lock checks are centered on direct object identity. Object Group operations can be reasoned about separately from member lock state, which leaves room for indirect mutation of a locked Design Object through its group.

**Desired behavior:**
A Design Object Lock protects the object even when the user interacts with an Object Group that contains it. If an Object Group contains any locked member, editing operations that would mutate that group or its members are blocked. Read-only presentation such as rendering and hover may continue as long as it does not mutate the locked member.

**Key interfaces:**
- SceneStore lock lookup for Design Objects and Object Groups
- Scene interaction hit testing and band selection
- Scene Edit transactions for drag, delete, ungroup, reorder, and stamp operations
- Object Stamp and Plant Spacing source validation
- Target and selection behavior for grouped Design Objects

**Out of scope:**
- Changing the embedded persistence contract for Design Object Locks
- Adding visual lock indicators
- Changing Layer lock semantics
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] A group containing a locked placed plant, zone, or annotation cannot be selected for editing through ordinary hit testing or band selection.
- [ ] Drag, delete, ungroup, reorder, Object Stamp, and other mutating group operations reject Object Groups that contain locked members.
- [ ] Object Groups with no locked members keep existing behavior.
- [ ] A directly locked Object Group remains protected even when its members are unlocked.
- [ ] Regression tests cover locked members across all grouped Design Object types and verify no false positives for missing or unlocked members.
- [ ] Agent docs are updated if the implementation adds or changes lock-related SceneStore or Scene Edit rules.
close_reason: Enforced effective group locks when any existing member Design Object is locked, covering selection, select-all, Object Stamp, duplicate, delete, ungroup, and reorder with focused tests plus full frontend gates.

title: Persist direct Design Object Locks through SceneStore
description:
## Slice Summary
Persist direct Design Object Lock state by embedding a lock field on every Design Object and moving direct lock/unlock behavior behind SceneStore and Scene Edit. A directly locked Design Object should save, reopen, undo, redo, and block direct canvas editing operations.

## User Value
A user can lock a placed plant, zone, annotation, or object group and trust that the lock survives normal Design save/load behavior instead of being a transient runtime mirror.

## Out of Scope
Indirect Object Group member lock invariants are handled by a follow-up slice. This slice should not add new lock UI chrome or redesign layer locking.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** feature
**Summary:** Persist direct Design Object Locks as embedded Design Object state owned by SceneStore.

**Current behavior:**
Design Object lock state is represented as transient runtime mirror state. It participates in parts of Scene Edit and history behavior, but it is not embedded in the saved Design Object data and does not have one SceneStore-owned authority.

**Desired behavior:**
Every Design Object carries a saved `locked` boolean. Older Designs load with all Design Objects unlocked. New saves write explicit lock state. Direct lock and unlock commands update Design Object state through Scene Edit so dirty state, undo, redo, save, and load all observe the same authority. Direct selection, transformation, deletion, select-all, band selection, Object Stamp source selection, and Plant Spacing source selection respect direct Design Object Locks.

**Key interfaces:**
- Shared Design file contract for placed plants, zones, annotations, and object groups
- SceneStore hydration, serialization, snapshots, and restore behavior
- Scene Edit transactions and Scene History commands
- Canvas command, query, and document surfaces
- File-format round-trip behavior for old and new Designs

**Out of scope:**
- Indirect Object Group member lock invariants
- New visual lock indicators or lock-management UI
- Changes to Layer lock semantics
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] The shared Design contract exposes a required `locked: boolean` field for placed plants, zones, annotations, and object groups in generated frontend types.
- [ ] Rust deserialization defaults missing Design Object lock fields to `false` so old `.canopi` files load without migration errors.
- [ ] SceneStore hydrates, clones, snapshots, restores, and serializes embedded Design Object lock state for all Design Object types.
- [ ] Direct lock and unlock commands write through Scene Edit and participate in dirty state, undo, redo, save, and reopen behavior.
- [ ] Direct selection, band selection, select-all, deletion, Object Stamp source selection, and Plant Spacing source selection respect directly locked Design Objects.
- [ ] Production code no longer depends on a global `lockedObjectIds` runtime mirror for Design Object lock authority.
- [ ] Required generated-contract and frontend quality gates pass, including focused SceneStore, Scene Edit, and interaction regression tests.
close_reason: Moved Design Object lock state into SceneStore persisted entities, removed the lockedObjectIds production mirror, regenerated shared bindings, and verified with frontend/Rust gates.

title: Collapse Timeline Action canvas into a host-model seam
description:
## Slice Summary
Deepen the Timeline Action canvas module so the canvas host consumes one compact host model for rendering, events, and overlays while interaction ordering, document mutation, and Target Presentation writes stay internal.

## User Value
Timeline Action behavior stays the same, but future timeline maintenance has one narrow interface instead of a wide set of caller-visible implementation details.

## Out of Scope
This slice should not change Timeline Action behavior, visual design, Planning Projection semantics, or renderer output.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Deepen the Timeline Action canvas module behind a compact host-model interface.

**Current behavior:**
The Timeline Action canvas host consumes a wide surface: planning rows, layout, row offsets, render dependencies, cached geometry, event handlers, tooltip and popover state, cleanup behavior, and document mutation callbacks are exposed separately. The implementation has useful internal seams, but the caller-facing interface is still close to the implementation.

**Desired behavior:**
The Timeline Action canvas module exposes a compact host model for the canvas host: canvas wiring, container wiring, render inputs, event wiring, and overlay state. Interaction ordering, document listeners, popover lifecycle, Target Presentation writes, selection cleanup, and document mutation orchestration stay internal to the Timeline Action canvas module. Pure Timeline rendering remains separately testable and unchanged.

**Key interfaces:**
- Timeline Action canvas workbench
- Timeline Action interaction frame and interaction workbench
- Timeline Action document editing and popover orchestration
- Planning Projection for Timeline Action read models
- Target Presentation for Timeline Action hover and selection
- Canvas2D Timeline renderer

**Out of scope:**
- Timeline Action behavior changes
- Timeline visual redesign
- Planning Projection semantic changes
- Replacing the existing Canvas2D renderer
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] The Timeline Action canvas host consumes one compact host model instead of independently wiring render dependencies, geometry cache, event handlers, tooltip state, popover state, and cleanup behavior.
- [ ] Timeline Action interaction ordering, document listeners, popover lifecycle, Target Presentation writes, selection cleanup, and document mutation orchestration stay internal to the Timeline Action canvas module.
- [ ] Existing Timeline Action add, edit, delete, hover, select, drag, resize, pan, zoom, auto-scroll, and popover behavior is preserved.
- [ ] Focused source-boundary tests prevent the canvas host from re-importing interaction-frame, interaction math, document mutation, or Target Presentation internals.
- [ ] Focused behavior tests cover cleanup and at least one representative Timeline Action edit path through the deepened interface.
- [ ] Required frontend typecheck and focused Vitest coverage pass.
close_reason: Shipped Timeline Action canvas host model seam: InteractiveTimeline now consumes container/canvas/renderer/overlay facets from useTimelineActionCanvasHostModel, source-boundary tests prevent renderer/settings/raw workbench reimports, mounted behavior tests cover host cleanup and edit-save path. Gates: focused Timeline Vitest, full npm test, npx tsc --noEmit --pretty false.

title: Apply Visual Footprint curve to Placed Plant dots
description:
## Slice Summary
Apply the resolved Visual Footprint policy to symbolic Placed Plant markers in default Plant Size Mode.

## User Value
Designers see default Placed Plant markers scale smoothly with zoom, stay visible when zoomed out, and become slightly smaller than today at close zoom.

## Out of Scope
Do not change persisted Design data, canopy spread geometry for known canopy data, Zone geometry, Annotation text behavior, stack badge grouping semantics, or general renderer stroke normalization.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** feature
**Summary:** Apply a smooth Visual Footprint curve to symbolic Placed Plant dots.

**Current behavior:**
Default Plant Size Mode represents Placed Plants with a hybrid symbolic dot whose screen size uses hard floor and cap behavior. This can make zooming feel mechanical, and close zoom markers are larger than the resolved target. Canopy spread mode is physically meaningful when canopy spread data is known, while missing canopy data currently falls back through legacy symbolic sizing.

**Desired behavior:**
Default Plant Size Mode dots use one shared Visual Footprint calculation based on absolute viewport scale in pixels per meter. The curve should be smooth with soft screen-readable limits: about 2px minimum, about 6.75px asymptotic maximum, roughly 2.2px at 1 px/m, and roughly 6.3px at 200 px/m. Rendering, hit testing, band selection bounds, grouping bounds, zoom-to-fit bounds, and missing-canopy fallback should consume the same computed footprint. Known canopy spread remains physical geometry and bypasses the symbolic curve.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant Size Mode presentation state
- Plant presentation context and entries
- Scene-side hit testing and bounds
- Scene zoom-to-fit bounds
- Renderer snapshots for Placed Plants

**Out of scope:**
- Changing persisted Design data or adding migrations
- Changing known canopy spread geometry
- Changing Zone geometry or Annotation text behavior
- Changing stack badge grouping semantics or badge placement
- Normalizing general renderer strokes and outlines
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Default Plant Size Mode dot radius follows a smooth absolute-scale Visual Footprint curve with about 2px minimum and about 6.75px asymptotic maximum.
- [ ] At representative scales, focused tests cover approximate radius targets around 1 px/m, 5 px/m, 10 px/m, 50 px/m, and 200 px/m.
- [ ] Rendering, hit testing, band selection bounds, grouping bounds, and zoom-to-fit bounds all consume the same symbolic plant footprint.
- [ ] Known canopy spread mode remains physical geometry and does not use the symbolic curve.
- [ ] Missing canopy spread fallback uses the symbolic Visual Footprint curve.
- [ ] No persisted Design schema, generated bindings, or migration changes are introduced.
- [ ] Focused frontend tests and `cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit` pass.
close_reason: Applied smooth absolute-scale Visual Footprint sizing for default Placed Plant dots and missing-canopy fallback while preserving known canopy geometry. Added focused coverage for representative radii, hit bounds, scene bounds, band selection, and Plant Spacing ghost sizing. Verified with npm test -- --run src/__tests__/plant-presentation.test.ts src/__tests__/camera-controller.test.ts src/__tests__/hit-testing.test.ts; npm test; npx tsc --noEmit; git diff --check.

title: Normalize screen-readable canvas presentation aids
description:
## Slice Summary
Align canvas presentation aids so strokes, outlines, labels, badges, and annotation selection affordances stay screen-readable across zoom and renderer backends.

## User Value
Designers see consistent visual weight for presentation aids while zooming, without physical Design Object geometry being distorted.

## Out of Scope
Do not change symbolic plant dot sizing, stack badge grouping semantics, persisted Design data, map projection behavior, grid/ruler chrome, or Zone physical geometry.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** feature
**Summary:** Normalize screen-readable canvas presentation aids across zoom and renderer backends.

**Current behavior:**
Some canvas presentation aids are already screen-readable, while others can scale as world geometry depending on renderer or object type. This creates inconsistent visual weight when zooming and can make Pixi and Canvas2D diverge for equivalent Design Objects.

**Desired behavior:**
Presentation aids remain screen-readable across zoom without changing physical Design Object geometry. Zone strokes, selected and highlighted outlines, plant strokes and highlight rings, annotation selection rectangles, labels, stack badge visuals, handles if present, and Zone Measurement text should keep stable screen-readable visual weight. Zones and known canopy spread remain physical geometry; Annotation text remains readable presentation anchored to a design position.

**Key interfaces:**
- Renderer snapshots and renderer backend parity
- Zone rendering and highlighting
- Plant stroke and highlight rendering
- Annotation text bounds and selection outlines
- Selection labels and Zone Measurement overlays

**Out of scope:**
- Changing symbolic Placed Plant dot sizing or Visual Footprint curve
- Changing stack badge grouping semantics
- Changing persisted Design data, map projection behavior, grid/ruler chrome, or Zone physical geometry
- Redesigning colors, typography, or general canvas visual style
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Zone strokes and selected/highlighted outlines maintain stable screen-readable widths across zoom in both renderer backends.
- [ ] Plant strokes and highlight rings maintain stable screen-readable widths around the computed plant footprint.
- [ ] Annotation selection rectangles use the same visible text bounds as rendering, hit testing, band selection, grouping, and zoom-to-fit.
- [ ] Labels, stack badge visuals, handles if present, and Zone Measurement text remain screen-readable and do not scale as physical geometry.
- [ ] Physical geometry for Zones and known canopy spread remains unchanged.
- [ ] Focused renderer or presentation tests cover backend parity for the changed presentation aids.
- [ ] Focused frontend tests and `cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit` pass.
close_reason: Normalized Pixi Zone and Placed Plant stroke widths to screen-readable pixel weights across viewport scale, including viewport-only updates. Added Pixi renderer coverage for zone and plant stroke widths at multiple zoom levels. Verified with npm test -- --run src/__tests__/pixi-scene.test.ts; npm test; npx tsc --noEmit; git diff --check.

title: Keep stack badges anchored to current plant footprint
description:
## Slice Summary
Keep stack badges attached to Placed Plant markers after symbolic marker sizing becomes zoom-aware.

## User Value
Designers still get count badges for Placed Plants whose centers collapse together, and badges stay visually attached to the current marker size.

## Out of Scope
Do not badge ordinary Visual Footprint overlap, change the center-collapse threshold unless tests show it must be restored, or redesign badge styling.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** task
**Summary:** Keep stack badges anchored to the current Placed Plant Visual Footprint.

**Current behavior:**
Stack badge offsets are tied to legacy fixed dot-size assumptions. Once symbolic Placed Plant dots use a zoom-aware Visual Footprint curve, badge placement can drift from the marker size even if center-collapse grouping still behaves correctly.

**Desired behavior:**
Stack badges continue to indicate Placed Plants whose centers collapse to nearly the same screen position. Badge placement should derive from the current plant visual radius so the badge remains visually attached to the marker across zoom levels and Plant Size Mode fallback cases. Ordinary overlap between symbolic Visual Footprints should not create stack badges by itself.

**Key interfaces:**
- Plant presentation entries
- Stack badge decision model
- Renderer badge drawing for Placed Plants
- Scene-side plant presentation tests

**Out of scope:**
- Changing the meaning of stack badges from center collapse to visual overlap
- Redesigning badge styling, text, color, or count formatting
- Changing the Visual Footprint curve itself
- Changing selection labels or hover tooltips
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Stack badges still appear for Placed Plants whose centers collapse within the existing screen-space proximity rule.
- [ ] Stack badges do not appear merely because two symbolic plant Visual Footprints overlap at ordinary dense spacing.
- [ ] Badge center offsets are derived from the current plant visual radius, not a fixed legacy dot radius.
- [ ] Badge placement remains correct at representative low, normal, and high zoom scales.
- [ ] Pixi and Canvas2D badge drawing remain visually consistent with the shared badge decision data.
- [ ] Focused frontend tests and `cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit` pass.
close_reason: Updated stack badge placement to derive badge offsets from the current Placed Plant Visual Footprint while preserving center-collapse grouping semantics. Added coverage for footprint-derived badge centers and ordinary Visual Footprint overlap not creating badges. Verified with npm test -- --run src/__tests__/plant-presentation.test.ts; npm test; npx tsc --noEmit; git diff --check.

title: Remember bottom panel height per tab
description: Bottom Panel Tab heights should be remembered independently for Timeline, Budget, and Consortium. First-use defaults should fit the base Timeline and Consortium rows, while legacy 200px defaults should be treated as unset rather than as a user-resized preference.
acceptance_criteria: Timeline, Budget, and Consortium each have independent persisted heights after manual resize. Tabs with no explicit height use compact first-use defaults. Existing single bottom_panel_height values migrate so 200px is treated as unset and other values seed all tabs. Focused settings projection tests cover hydration, snapshotting, and legacy normalization.
close_reason: Implemented per-tab Bottom Panel Tab height preferences with nullable first-use defaults, legacy bottom_panel_height migration, regenerated settings bindings, focused frontend coverage, Rust migration tests, and agent docs updates. Verified with focused Vitest, npx tsc --noEmit, npm run check:types, cargo fmt --all -- --check, CANOPI_SKIP_BUNDLED_DB=1 cargo check --workspace, CANOPI_SKIP_BUNDLED_DB=1 cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test --workspace, and git diff --check.

title: Fix Species Catalog filter row height reservation
description:
## Slice Summary
Fix the always-visible Species Catalog Filter rows so wrapped choice ribbons reserve vertical space in their own row instead of painting behind the next filter row.

## User Value
Users resizing the Species Catalog Workbench sidebar can see every Climate Zone ribbon, including Boreal, without wrapped ribbons overlapping Habit or any following filter row.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Species Catalog filter semantics, option ordering, backend filtering behavior, sidebar width persistence, or redesign the More Filters panel.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Make always-visible Species Catalog Filter rows reserve height for wrapped choice ribbons.

**Current behavior:**
At very narrow Species Catalog Workbench sidebar widths, Climate Zone choice ribbons wrap, but the Climate Zone row does not reserve enough vertical space for every wrapped line. The final ribbon, such as Boreal, can paint behind the following Habit row instead of forcing Climate Zone to become taller.

**Desired behavior:**
Each always-visible choice filter row participates in normal vertical flow and reserves enough height for every wrapped ribbon. Climate Zone may become three or more ribbon lines at narrow widths, then shrink as width increases. Wrapped ribbons must never overlap following filter rows. More Filters should remain the reference behavior: chip groups wrap naturally and their parent content grows around them.

**Key interfaces:**
- Species Catalog Workbench sidebar
- Always-visible Species Catalog Filter strip
- Choice filter row and ribbon/chip presentation
- Filter region height cap and scrolling behavior
- Frontend regression coverage for filter-row overlap

**Out of scope:**
- Changing Species Catalog request or backend filter contracts
- Changing Climate Zone values, translations, or option ordering
- Changing saved sidebar width persistence
- Redesigning More Filters
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] At narrow Species Catalog Workbench sidebar widths, Climate Zone reserves enough height for all wrapped ribbons, including Boreal.
- [ ] No wrapped choice ribbon paints behind Habit or any following always-visible filter row.
- [ ] Other always-visible choice filter rows keep natural wrapping behavior and reserve their wrapped height.
- [ ] The combined filter region remains the only capped/scrollable filter container; individual choice rows do not rely on visible overflow to expose wrapped content.
- [ ] Existing sidebar default width and saved width persistence behavior remain unchanged.
- [ ] More Filters chip wrapping behavior is not regressed.
- [ ] Focused frontend regression coverage protects the row-height reservation behavior, preferably through rendered geometry; if the local test environment cannot compute layout, the test must explicitly guard against the layout combination that allowed wrapped ribbons to escape their row.
- [ ] Required frontend checks are run, including focused Vitest coverage and `cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit`.
close_reason: Implemented flex-based Species Catalog filter rows so wrapped choice ribbons reserve row height; verified with focused filter-strip Vitest coverage and npx tsc --noEmit.

title: Fix Species Catalog filter strip row wrapping
description:
## Slice Summary
Fix the always-visible Species Catalog Filter strip so choice rows grow and shrink naturally as the Species Catalog Workbench sidebar width changes.

## User Value
Users resizing the Species Catalog Workbench can see all visible Climate Zone and other choice ribbons without rows appearing fixed at one or two lines.

## Out of Scope
Do not change Species Catalog request contracts, filter semantics, filter option ordering, sidebar width persistence, or the More Filters panel behavior except as needed to preserve shared wrapping expectations.
design:
## Agent Brief
**Category:** bug
**Summary:** Make always-visible Species Catalog Filter choice rows resize naturally with the Species Catalog Workbench sidebar.

**Current behavior:**
The always-visible Species Catalog Filter strip appears to keep Climate Zone at a fixed two-row height across practical sidebar width changes. Other choice rows can also appear locked to a single row. The implementation relies on a parent strip layout that does not behave like the natural wrapping chip groups used in More Filters, so row-height changes are not reliably visible when the panel width changes.

**Desired behavior:**
Each always-visible Species Catalog Filter row owns its own height. Choice rows wrap like More Filters chip groups: narrower sidebars may produce more ribbon rows, wider sidebars may produce fewer rows, and all visible ribbons remain available. The combined filter region may remain capped and scrollable, but individual choice rows must not clip or force a fixed row count.

**Key interfaces:**
- Species Catalog Workbench filter strip projection
- Always-visible Species Catalog Filter rows
- Shared filter chip/ribbon presentation
- Species Catalog sidebar width and resize behavior
- Frontend layout regression tests for Species Catalog filters

**Out of scope:**
- Changing Species Catalog search request shape or backend filtering behavior
- Changing Climate Zone option values, labels, translations, or ordering
- Reworking the More Filters panel beyond preserving matching wrapping behavior
- Changing saved sidebar width persistence semantics
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Always-visible choice filter rows use a real per-row layout container that can grow independently with wrapped ribbons.
- [ ] Climate Zone shows all visible ribbons at narrow, default, and widened Species Catalog Workbench sidebar widths, with row height decreasing or increasing according to available width.
- [ ] Other always-visible choice rows keep the same natural wrapping behavior and do not clip to a fixed one-row height.
- [ ] The combined filter region remains capped or scrollable only at the region level; individual choice rows do not use fixed height, row-level max height, or overflow clipping to hide ribbons.
- [ ] Existing sidebar default width and saved width persistence behavior remain unchanged.
- [ ] Focused frontend regression coverage protects the filter-strip layout contract, including the absence of the old fixed-row pattern.
- [ ] Required frontend checks are run, including the focused Vitest coverage and `cd desktop/web && npx tsc --noEmit`.
close_reason: Fixed Species Catalog filter strip row wrapping by replacing the parent-grid/display-contents strip layout with real per-row containers that preserve natural flex-wrapped choice ribbons. Verified with npm test -- --run src/__tests__/filter-strip.test.tsx, npx tsc --noEmit, and git diff --check.

title: Remember responsive Species Catalog Workbench width
description: Reduce the default Species Catalog Workbench sidebar footprint and make its always-visible filters respond to the remembered panel width.
acceptance_criteria: First-use sidebar width defaults to clamp(320px, 35vw, 90vw) without a fixed pixel cap; explicit resize is remembered for the current and future app sessions through settings; always-visible Species Catalog Filter rows wrap to content based on actual panel width without clipping; design docs record the sidebar width and filter wrapping rules.
notes:
Reopened after design review: the filter area needs a combined natural-height region with an extreme-height cap; specs must distinguish filter rows, filter categories, and filter choices. Second diagnosis: real CSS-module probe shows choice rows reflow only when actual panel width changes. The first-use clamp(320px, 35vw, 520px) cap kept large viewports at 520px, where Climate Zone is exactly two rows and shorter rows are one row.
Reopened after user report: visible filter-row breakpoints still feel stuck because Climate Zone has seven choices and the strip grid uses an 80px minimum choice track, keeping it at two rows across common remembered/sidebar widths while shorter rows remain one row.
Reopened after user comparison with More Filters: More Filters chip rows use natural flex wrapping, while the always-visible strip still used equal-track grid layout, so the row height did not follow ribbon content like the working implementation.
close_reason: Aligned the always-visible Species Catalog Filter choice rows with the working More Filters ribbon behavior: removed equal-track grid layout and strip-only chip sizing, so rows naturally grow and shrink from flex-wrapped FilterChip content. Verified with npm test -- filter-strip, npx tsc --noEmit, git diff --check, and a temporary headless Firefox probe at 320/420/520/700px.

**Desired behavior:**

**Out of scope:**
- Implementing any extraction.
- Changing user-facing canvas behavior.
- Replacing SceneCanvasRuntime public surfaces.
- Re-litigating existing Scene Edit authority.
